seo.title: Gambling Disorder Treatment in St. Louis | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Evidence-based treatment for gambling disorder in St. Louis. CBT, motivational interviewing, and a certified gambling disorder counselor on staff. Get help today.

condition.name: Gambling Disorder
condition.schemaDescription: Gambling disorder is a behavioral addiction characterized by persistent and recurrent problematic gambling behavior that causes significant impairment or distress, recognized in the DSM-5 as sharing core features with substance use disorders.

hero.headline: Gambling Disorder
hero.intro: Gambling disorder is the only non-substance behavioral addiction officially recognized in the DSM-5, and it belongs there. It shares the same underlying neurobiology as substance use disorders — the same dopamine-driven reward circuitry, the same patterns of tolerance and withdrawal-like states, the same experience of loss of control. It is real, it causes serious harm, and it responds to the same evidence-based treatments that work for substance use disorders. If gambling has taken over a part of your life you want back, treatment is available.

criteria.heading: What Is Gambling Disorder?
criteria.description: The DSM-5 defines gambling disorder as persistent and recurrent problematic gambling behavior leading to significant impairment or distress, with at least 4 of the following occurring within a 12-month period:

items.label: Preoccupation
items.description: Being preoccupied with gambling — constantly thinking about past gambling experiences, planning future gambling, or thinking about ways to get money to gamble.

items.label: Tolerance
items.description: Needing to gamble with increasingly larger amounts of money to achieve the desired level of excitement.

items.label: Restlessness or irritability
items.description: Feeling restless, irritable, or anxious when trying to cut down or stop gambling — a withdrawal-like state.

items.label: Failed attempts to control gambling
items.description: Having made repeated unsuccessful efforts to cut back, control, or stop gambling.

items.label: Gambling to escape
items.description: Gambling to escape problems, relieve helplessness, guilt, anxiety, or depression — using gambling as a coping mechanism.

items.label: Chasing losses
items.description: After losing money gambling, returning another day to try to win it back — 'chasing' losses.

items.label: Lying to conceal gambling
items.description: Lying to family members, a therapist, or others to conceal the extent of gambling involvement.

items.label: Jeopardizing significant relationships or opportunities
items.description: Gambling has jeopardized or caused loss of a significant relationship, job, or educational or career opportunity.

items.label: Relying on others financially
items.description: Relying on others — family, friends, employers — to provide money to relieve desperate financial situations caused by gambling.

criteria.note: 4–5 criteria indicate mild gambling disorder; 6–7 moderate; 8–9 severe. Gambling disorder is not diagnosed when gambling behavior is better explained by a manic episode.

howItFeels.heading: What Gambling Disorder Actually Looks Like
paragraphs: The experience of gambling disorder is often described as 'the chase' — not primarily the desire to win money, but the pursuit of a particular mental state. The action, the anticipation, the brief suspension of everything else. This is why winning often does not stop gambling; it funds more of it. The goal, neurologically, is the experience itself — and the brain adapts to require more of it over time, the same way it adapts to substances.

Near-misses are a particularly powerful driver of continued gambling. Slot machines and other modern gambling products are engineered around the neurological fact that near-misses activate the brain's reward circuitry almost as strongly as actual wins — and they intensify the urge to continue. This is not a design flaw; it is the intended effect. Understanding this can help reduce the shame of not being able to 'just walk away.'

Financial crisis is often what forces the issue into the open. By the time someone seeks help, the financial damage is frequently severe — depleted savings, borrowed money, unpaid bills, loans from family members who may not know the full extent. This layer of practical devastation compounds the shame and can make it hard to see a way forward. It is important to name this clearly: financial harm is a consequence of the disorder, not evidence of bad character. It can be addressed, and recovery does not require resolving the finances first.

Online gambling has changed the landscape profoundly. The combination of constant availability, anonymity, ease of access from home, and the specific design of digital gambling products has increased both the prevalence of gambling disorder and its severity. People who might have gambled occasionally in person now have access to casino-quality products at 2 a.m. on their phone. This context matters clinically — online gambling is associated with faster escalation and higher rates of financial harm than venue-based gambling.

treatmentSection.heading: How We Treat Gambling Disorder
treatmentSection.intro: We have a certified gambling disorder counselor on staff and significant clinical experience with the specific patterns and consequences of problematic gambling. Treatment is evidence-based, individualized, and attentive to both the gambling behavior and the financial, relational, and mental health consequences that almost always accompany it.

treatments.name: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
treatments.description: CBT is the most evidence-supported treatment for gambling disorder. It addresses the cognitive distortions that sustain gambling — the gambler's fallacy, the illusion of control, the belief that winning is always just one more bet away — as well as the emotional and situational triggers that drive the urge to gamble. CBT also builds practical relapse prevention skills.

treatments.name: Motivational Interviewing
treatments.description: Many people who seek help for gambling disorder are ambivalent — the gambling has caused real harm, but it has also served real functions. Motivational interviewing helps you explore that ambivalence honestly and clarify your own reasons for change, without pressure or judgment. This often accelerates engagement with the harder work of behavioral change.

treatments.name: Treating Co-Occurring Depression and Anxiety
treatments.description: Depression and anxiety co-occur with gambling disorder at very high rates — often preceding it, often worsened by it. People frequently gamble to escape difficult emotional states, which means that untreated depression or anxiety undermines gambling recovery. We assess and treat co-occurring conditions as part of the same care, not as a separate referral.

treatments.name: Gamblers Anonymous and Mutual Support
treatments.description: Gamblers Anonymous offers peer support, community, and a structured recovery program for people with gambling disorder. It works well for many people, particularly in combination with professional treatment. We do not require attendance, but we support it and can help you connect with local meetings.

treatments.name: Financial Counseling Referral
treatments.description: The financial consequences of gambling disorder are often severe and require specialized attention. While we do not provide financial counseling directly, we actively support connecting clients with financial counselors and credit counseling services as part of a comprehensive recovery plan. Addressing the financial wreckage is important for sustained recovery — it reduces ongoing stress and begins to restore the practical stability that recovery requires.

faqs.question: Is gambling disorder a real addiction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gambling disorder is the only behavioral addiction recognized in the DSM-5, and it was included based on substantial evidence that it shares the core neurobiological features of substance use disorders — the same dopamine-driven reward circuitry, the same tolerance and withdrawal-like states, the same patterns of escalation and loss of control. Brain imaging studies show nearly identical activation patterns in people with gambling disorder and people with substance use disorders when exposed to cues related to their respective compulsions. The fact that no substance is involved does not make it less real.

faqs.question: The financial damage feels unrecoverable. Does that have to be addressed in treatment?
faqs.answer: Not all at once, and not as a precondition of recovery. The financial consequences of gambling disorder are often severe, and they are a legitimate part of the clinical picture — ongoing financial stress is a relapse trigger, and ignoring it creates a background of anxiety that makes sustained recovery harder. But the goal of treatment is stabilization and behavior change first. The finances can be addressed in stages, often with help from financial counselors or credit counseling services. We can help connect you with resources. People do recover from significant financial harm — it is not a permanent state.

faqs.question: Is there medication for gambling disorder?
faqs.answer: There is no FDA-approved medication specifically for gambling disorder. However, some medications show promise in research settings. Naltrexone — the same opioid antagonist used in alcohol use disorder — has shown the most consistent evidence for reducing gambling urges and behavior, likely because it modulates the opioid component of the reward response. Antidepressants may be appropriate if co-occurring depression or anxiety is present. Medication is not a primary treatment for gambling disorder the way it is for opioid use disorder, but it can play a supporting role in some cases.

faqs.question: How is online gambling different from casino gambling in terms of risk?
faqs.answer: Online gambling carries substantially higher risk for several reasons. It is available at any hour, from anywhere, with no friction — there is no travel, no social exposure, no obvious break in the behavior. Digital gambling products are specifically engineered to maximize engagement and minimize natural stopping points. Stakes can escalate rapidly and privately, without the social checks that exist in physical venues. And because it happens in the home, often late at night, it is easier to hide from family members. The combination of accessibility, anonymity, and design creates conditions for faster escalation and more severe financial harm than most venue-based gambling.

faqs.question: My family member gambles. What can we do?
faqs.answer: You can do quite a bit, even if your family member is not ready to seek help themselves. Gam-Anon is a support organization specifically for family members of people with gambling disorder — it provides community, perspective, and practical guidance. We also work with families directly: helping you understand the nature of gambling disorder, how to communicate about it effectively, how to set boundaries that protect you without simply enabling continued gambling, and how to support recovery without taking responsibility for it. If you are in a financial crisis because of someone else's gambling, that is a legitimate emergency that deserves immediate attention regardless of where your family member is in their own process.
